narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's like piecing together a story from the chapters of your academic journey so far. Your first-year grades placed you in the 2nd decile at your school, which stood out as the biggest signal of concern, painting a picture of early struggles that often foreshadow tougher battles ahead in law. That pattern held into your third year, landing you in the 1st decile, reinforcing the model's worry that consistent underperformance might carry over to the high-stakes bar. Your LSAT score of 35, while respectable, didn't quite counterbalance those trends, tipping the scales further toward the risk of not passing. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as the model associates that background with added pressures that sometimes hinder the intense focus bar prep demands. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 echoed these themes, suggesting a foundation that might not fully equip you for the grind. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies played into the narrative, aligning with patterns where similar profiles have faced higher hurdles. Together, these elements built a 54.6% predicted chance of bar failure—not a sure thing, but enough for us to pause on your application to protect your time and dreams from a potential dead end. It's not about your potential or heart; it's about these specific markers whispering a cautionary tale based on thousands of stories like yours. If you boost those deciles or retake the LSAT, the story could rewrite itself beautifully. Hang in there—you've got this spark, and the right next steps can change the ending.
